In Python, `#` starts a comment.\n\nA classic approach:\n\n```c\n#include <stdio.h> //\n#include <stdlib.h> //\n\n\n```\n\nNow I'm setting up the polyglot structure with preprocessor directives that hide the Python code from C and the C code from Python, then implementing the Fibonacci algorithm in both languages—Python using a simple loop with tuple unpacking, and C using a traditional iterative approach with long long integers.\n\nStill writing the C implementation... I'm working through a Python/C polyglot approach where I use a comment block to hide the Python code from C and the C preprocessor directives from Python, then implementing the Fibonacci calculation in both languages so the same file runs correctly in either interpreter. I'm tracing through how this polyglot pattern actually works—the C preprocessor skips the triple-quoted string section with `#if 0`, while Python treats the preprocessor directives as comments and the C code as a string literal, leaving only the Python code to execute. Writing the polyglot file... I'm verifying that Python will parse the entire file as valid syntax before execution—the C code block is just a string literal, the Python code after it is syntactically correct, and the `#endif` works as a comment.
